summary: The Greek versions of Daniel (Old Greek and Theodotion) exhibit pronounced eschatological sharpening and messianic heightening, particularly in Daniel 7–12, amplifying end-time scenarios and messianic figures. The Targum systematically avoids anthropomorphisms in theophanies and introduces proto-rabbinic exegetical expansions that identify apocalyptic figures with specific historical or eschatological referents. Harmonization with other biblical texts is evident across all traditions, especially in synchronizing Daniel's vision chronology with established prophetic frameworks.
summary_plain: Translators and scribes modified Daniel to make visions of the end times more vivid and explicit, to remove language that made God seem too human, and to align Daniel's prophecies with other parts of Scripture. These changes reflect early Jewish and Christian efforts to interpret Daniel's cryptic visions through the lens of their own expectations about the Messiah and the end of the world.

revisions.mt_reading: and the appearance of the fourth is like a son of the gods (bar-elahin)
revisions.revised_reading: and the appearance of the fourth is like a son of God (huios theou, singular)
revisions.evidence: The MT's polytheistic-sounding 'son of the gods' is rendered as singular 'son of God' in OG and Theodotion, eliminating polytheistic overtones and introducing potential messianic/Christological reading. This is not merely lexical equivalence but theological disambiguation in a post-exilic monotheistic context, amplified by early Christian appropriation of the phrase.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Hebrew phrase 'son of the gods' sounded too polytheistic, so Greek translators changed it to 'son of God' (singular), which could then be read as a reference to the Messiah or Christ.
revisions.counter_arguments: Could reflect standard Greek rendering of Aramaic plural of majesty; Christian messianic reading may be secondary reception rather than translational intent.

revisions.mt_reading: one like a son of man (ke-bar enash) coming with the clouds of heaven
revisions.revised_reading: as (hōs) the Son of Man (ho huios tou anthrōpou) coming with the clouds — OG intensifies the titular quality
revisions.evidence: While MT 'like a son of man' is indefinite and simile-like, the Greek traditions (especially Theodotion's use of the article) move toward titular/messianic identification. The LXX/OG interpretive tradition reflects pre-Christian Jewish messianism that reads this figure as a specific eschatological agent, not merely 'one who appears human.' This is consistent with 1 Enoch's appropriation of the Son of Man title from Daniel 7.
revisions.evidence_plain: The vague Hebrew phrase 'one like a human being' became 'the Son of Man' in Greek, a title that both Jews and Christians came to understand as referring to a specific messianic figure who would come at the end of time.
scholarly_citations: Schaper, Eschatology in the Greek Psalter, pp. 45-47
revisions.counter_arguments: Greek article usage may be stylistic; titular reading is clearer in NT reception than in LXX Daniel itself.

revisions.mt_reading: the Ancient of Days took his seat. His clothing was white as snow; the hair of his head like pure wool
revisions.revised_reading: The Ancient of Days is seated on the throne of His glory... His garment white like snow, and like pure wool the appearance of the crown of His head
revisions.evidence: The Targum systematically mediates direct bodily description of God: 'took his seat' becomes passive divine presence ('is seated'), 'hair' becomes 'appearance of the crown,' avoiding literal divine corporeality. This reflects proto-rabbinic anxiety about anthropomorphism in synagogue reading, consistent with Targum Onkelos's treatment of divine physicality. The shift from direct description to 'appearance' introduces a metaphysical buffer.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic translation avoids saying God literally has hair or sits down, instead describing what His glory looks like or how it appears, reflecting discomfort with making God sound too physical.
scholarly_citations: van der Kooij, Oracle of Tyre, pp. 89-91
revisions.counter_arguments: Some scholars see this as stylistic elaboration rather than strict anthropomorphism avoidance; the Targum retains significant anthropomorphic language elsewhere.

revisions.mt_reading: Seventy weeks are decreed... to bring in everlasting righteousness... an anointed one, a prince (mashiach nagid)
revisions.revised_reading: OG intensifies the finality and cosmic scope: 'to seal vision and prophet' becomes 'to seal up vision and prophecy' — emphasizing cessation and eschatological closure
revisions.evidence: The OG rendering of Daniel 9:24 amplifies the eschatological closure of prophetic revelation ('seal up' suggesting finality) and cosmic scope of the seventy-weeks schema. The chronology is rendered more deterministically, consistent with apocalyptic periodization in Second Temple Judaism. While not as dramatic as other revisions, the cumulative effect is to heighten the sense that these weeks constitute an irrevocable divine timetable for history's end.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek version made the prophecy of the seventy weeks sound more final and absolute, emphasizing that this was God's fixed schedule for the end of the world and the closing of all prophecy.
scholarly_citations: Meadowcroft, Aramaic Daniel and Greek Daniel, pp. 234-237
revisions.counter_arguments: Differences may reflect variant Semitic Vorlage rather than theological revision; the eschatological framework is already present in MT.

revisions.mt_reading: the abomination that makes desolate (shiqquts meshomem)
revisions.revised_reading: the abomination of desolation (bdelugma tēs erēmōseōs) — standardized phraseology used also in 1 Maccabees 1:54
revisions.evidence: The Greek phrase 'abomination of desolation' becomes a technical term harmonized across Daniel 9:27, 11:31, 12:11, and intertextually with 1 Maccabees. This standardization reflects post-Antiochan Epiphanes interpretive tradition that identifies the phrase with desecration of the Temple, creating lexical consistency where MT shows variation. The harmonization facilitates typological reuse in Jewish and Christian eschatology (cf. Mark 13:14).
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek translators used the same phrase every time Daniel mentions the 'abomination,' creating a catchphrase that linked Daniel's prophecies to the actual desecration of the Temple and made it easier to apply the prophecy to future events.
revisions.counter_arguments: Standardization may be natural translation practice rather than theological harmonization; the conceptual link is already present in MT.

revisions.mt_reading: many of those who sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake, some to everlasting life, and some to shame and everlasting contempt
revisions.revised_reading: Targum expands: 'many of those who are asleep in the dust... the righteous to everlasting life and the wicked to be a reproach' — specifying moral categories and adding interpretive glosses
revisions.evidence: The Targum introduces explicit moral categorization ('righteous'/'wicked') absent in MT's ambiguous 'some/some,' reflecting proto-rabbinic hermeneutics that systematize eschatological judgment. The expansion aligns with early rabbinic debates on resurrection and ensures clarity about who receives which fate, functioning as built-in halakhic commentary. This mirrors Targum's general tendency to disambiguate ambiguous eschatology.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic translation clarifies that 'the righteous' go to eternal life and 'the wicked' to eternal shame, making explicit the moral categories that the Hebrew left somewhat unclear.
revisions.counter_arguments: The clarification may be implicit in MT context; not all proto-rabbinic expansions constitute theological revision per se.

revisions.mt_reading: in the days of those kings the God of heaven will set up a kingdom that shall never be destroyed
revisions.revised_reading: OG and Theodotion intensify temporal specificity and cosmic scope: 'will raise up' (anastēsei) with resurrection overtones, 'a kingdom which shall never be destroyed unto the ages'
revisions.evidence: The Greek verb 'anastēsei' (raise up) carries resurrection connotations in Second Temple usage, adding eschatological depth to the establishment of the eternal kingdom. The phrase 'unto the ages' (eis tous aiōnas) intensifies the temporal infinity beyond MT's already strong language. This reflects Greek Daniel's tendency to amplify eschatological horizons and align vocabulary with resurrection theology emerging in Jewish apocalyptic literature.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek version uses a word for 'raise up' that also means 'resurrect,' and adds 'unto the ages,' making the promise of God's eternal kingdom sound even more cosmic and connected to the resurrection of the dead.
scholarly_citations: Meadowcroft, Aramaic Daniel and Greek Daniel, pp. 189-192
revisions.counter_arguments: Anastēsei may be standard vocabulary for establishing kingdoms without resurrection overtones; eschatological emphasis is already present in MT.

revisions.mt_reading: the prince (sar) of the kingdom of Persia... Michael, one of the chief princes
revisions.revised_reading: OG clarifies angelic hierarchies, using archōn consistently and adding interpretive glosses that align with developed angelology
revisions.evidence: The Greek tradition standardizes terminology for angelic princes (archontes) and provides interpretive clarity to the cosmic conflict framework, harmonizing with emerging Second Temple angelology found in 1 Enoch and Jubilees. The LXX's treatment of 'Michael, one of the chief princes' makes explicit the hierarchical structure of angelic armies, reducing ambiguity about the nature of these 'princes' and their relationship to earthly kingdoms.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek translators clarified that the 'princes' mentioned are angelic beings in a cosmic hierarchy, reflecting more developed ideas about angels that were emerging in Jewish thought at the time.
revisions.counter_arguments: Greek terminology may reflect standard equivalents rather than theological harmonization; the angelological framework is implicit in MT.

revisions.reference: Daniel 3:25 (Prayer of Azariah and Song of Three)
revisions.mt_reading: [The story moves directly from v. 23 to v. 24 with no content in the furnace]
revisions.revised_reading: LXX inserts extended prayer (Prayer of Azariah) and hymn (Song of the Three) between vv. 23-24, providing pious content and theodicy
revisions.evidence: The additions are not mere expansions but theological softening of the abrupt MT narrative that lacks explicit piety or theodicy in the furnace. The Prayer of Azariah provides confession and justification of exile, while the Song offers liturgical praise, transforming a miracle story into a devotional text. This reflects Hellenistic Jewish piety and the need to model appropriate prayer in extremis, consistent with additions in Esther and other LXX books.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek version adds long prayers and hymns sung by the three men in the furnace, giving them pious words to say and explaining why this disaster happened to God's people, which the Hebrew version lacks entirely.
scholarly_citations: Moore, Daniel, Esther and Jeremiah: The Additions (AB), pp. 31-38
revisions.counter_arguments: These may be independent liturgical compositions incorporated into Daniel rather than 'softening' revisions; their origin and intent remain debated.

revisions.mt_reading: he shall speak words against the Most High... and shall think to change the times and the law
revisions.revised_reading: Peshitta clarifies 'times and law' as 'appointed times and laws,' harmonizing with Levitical festival language (mo'adim)
revisions.evidence: The Syriac tradition interprets the ambiguous 'change times and law' through the lens of cultic calendar disputes, reflecting historical conflicts over festival observance (e.g., Qumran solar vs. lunar calendars, Maccabean conflicts). By specifying 'appointed times' (zabnē) the Peshitta harmonizes Daniel 7:25 with Pentateuchal feast legislation, making the blasphemy concrete: altering God's sacred calendar. This reflects Syriac Christian interest in regulating liturgical time.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Syriac version specifies that the evil ruler will try to change God's festival calendar and religious laws, making concrete what was vague in the Hebrew and connecting it to debates about when to celebrate holy days.
scholarly_citations: Gwynn, Remnants of the Later Syriac Versions, pp. 45-47
revisions.counter_arguments: This may be standard lexical choice rather than theological harmonization; the connection to mo'adim is contextually plausible even in MT.

dominant_strategy: Eschatological sharpening and messianic heightening dominate the Greek traditions of Daniel (OG and Theodotion), particularly in chapters 7–12. Translators amplified the apocalyptic intensity of Daniel's visions, made temporal frameworks more deterministic, and transformed ambiguous figures ('one like a son of man,' 'an anointed one') into recognizable messianic titles. This reflects Second Temple Judaism's growing systematization of eschatological expectation and the need to render Daniel's cryptic Aramaic visions intelligible within Hellenistic Jewish apocalyptic frameworks. The Targum, by contrast, prioritizes anthropomorphism avoidance and proto-rabbinic clarification, while the Peshitta shows liturgical harmonization.
overall_assessment: The textual traditions of Daniel reveal a sustained effort to render its cryptic apocalyptic visions theologically explicit and liturgically usable. The Greek versions, produced in the crucible of Hellenistic Judaism and early Christianity, intensify messianic and eschatological themes to align with emerging apocalyptic expectations (1 Enoch, 4 Ezra, early Christian typology). The Targum reflects proto-rabbinic concerns with anthropomorphism and moral clarity, disambiguating resurrection theology and angelic hierarchies. These revisions are not random but reflect the interpretive needs of tradent communities: Greek Daniel serves apocalyptic speculation and messianic identification; Targumic Daniel serves synagogue pedagogy and halakhic clarity. The pattern situates Daniel at the intersection of Jewish apocalypticism, proto-rabbinic exegesis, and early Christian appropriation, making it one of the most theologically revised books in the biblical corpus.
overall_plain: Translators and scribes revised Daniel to make its mysterious visions clearer and more useful for their communities. Greek versions emphasized the coming Messiah and the end of the world, while Aramaic versions made sure God didn't sound too human and clarified who would be resurrected and why. These changes show how different Jewish and Christian groups used Daniel to understand their own times and hopes for the future.

bibcrit_assessment.title: Greek Daniel Transforms Simile into Messianic Title
bibcrit_assessment.reasoning: The most significant finding is the transformation of 'one like a son of man' (MT simile, indefinite) into 'the Son of Man' (Greek title, definite) in Daniel 7:13. This is not translation but theological construction: the Greek traditions create a messianic title from a comparative description, enabling both pre-Christian Jewish apocalyptic (1 Enoch) and early Christian (Synoptic Gospels) appropriation. This single revision exemplifies how textual change generates new theological trajectories—the 'Son of Man' Christology depends on this Greek titular reading, which is not linguistically necessary from the Aramaic.
bibcrit_assessment.plain: The most important change is how Greek translators turned Daniel's vague description 'someone who looked like a human' into the title 'the Son of Man,' which both Jews and Christians then used to talk about the Messiah. This shows how a translation choice can create a whole new way of reading Scripture.
